surface.name: Authentication Quickstart through a working login or signup flow
surface.selection_basis: Stytch's official general Quickstart is an interactive router rather than a single implementation. It requires the developer to select UI, headless, or backend-only integration, then a framework, then Multi-tenant or Consumer Authentication. Each resulting guide requires a Stytch account and project credentials, but the public dashboard does not expose the account-creation or initial project-convergence sequence.

alternatives_considered.name: Stytch UI
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The Quickstart presents Stytch UI as a peer implementation type alongside Headless and Backend only, and it still requires a framework and Consumer versus Multi-tenant choice.

alternatives_considered.name: Headless frontend SDK
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is a peer route for developers who want their own UI; the general Quickstart does not rank it above the other implementation types.

alternatives_considered.name: Backend-only Node.js Consumer Authentication
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This route is fully documented after credentials and is preserved in the downstream gap, but it is not selected as Stytch's universal route.

alternatives_considered.name: Multi-tenant Authentication
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Stytch presents Multi-tenant Authentication for organization/member applications and Consumer Authentication for individual users as peer project types.

entry_point.developer_intent: Create a Stytch account and project, select an official authentication implementation, and test it until the guide's login or signup flow produces an authenticated session.
assumed_prior_state: No Stytch account, workspace, project, environment, API key, public token, redirect URL, authorized domain, SDK, application, or end-user session is assumed.
The developer has a local environment appropriate to the selected framework and can receive a test authentication email if the selected route uses email magic links.
No account signup, dashboard configuration, API-key retrieval, email send, authentication, or session creation was performed during research.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: Not established end to end by public official documentation
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: No universal Stytch first-success route can be established because required implementation choices remain open and the prerequisite account and initial project transition is hidden.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The public dashboard response is a client application shell and spinner; it does not establish account fields, verification, workspace creation, default project state, or successful access to project credentials.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The backend Consumer candidate demonstrates a protected dashboard response of `You're logged in.`, but the required account and project transition is not publicly established.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: Individual implementation guides demonstrate authenticated-session terminals, but they begin with project credentials and configuration. Those downstream terminals cannot establish an end-to-end first-user path while the prerequisite dashboard transition remains hidden and the general Quickstart does not choose a route.

prerequisites.requirement: A Stytch account created through dashboard signup.
A Consumer Authentication or Multi-tenant Authentication project and an environment with the credentials required by the selected route.
Use the environment's public_token for frontend SDKs or project_id and secret for backend API or SDK calls; each project has distinct Live and Test environment credentials.
A framework environment selected from the options Stytch exposes for the chosen implementation type.

primary_path.success_signal: Implementation type, framework, and authentication project-type controls are displayed.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: No navigation recovery behavior is documented.

primary_path.action: Select Stytch UI, Headless, or Backend only; select a supported framework; then select Multi-tenant Auth or Consumer Auth.
details: Stytch UI exposes React, Next.js, and Vanilla JS.
Headless also exposes iOS, Android, and React Native.
Backend only exposes Node, Python, Go, Ruby, and Java.
primary_path.input: Application architecture, framework, and tenant model
primary_path.success_signal: No single combination is selected by the official documentation for all developers.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The next actions change materially with the three choices.

primary_path.action: Sign up for the Stytch account required to obtain project credentials.
details: The API Keys documentation links account signup to the Stytch dashboard.
primary_path.output: The client-rendered dashboard account sequence begins.
primary_path.success_signal: No public official source establishes successful account, workspace, and initial-project convergence.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The public response exposes no signup recovery behavior.

candidate_path_gap.reason: A full candidate cannot cross the hidden account and project gate. For one downstream official candidate, the developer selects Backend only, Node, and Consumer Auth; has project_id and secret from a Stytch project and environment; adds `http://localhost:3000/authenticate` as a redirect URL; installs `stytch`, `express`, and `express-session`; stores STYTCH_PROJECT_ID and STYTCH_SECRET in .env; initializes the client and Express session middleware; creates POST `/login` to call email login_or_create with login and signup redirect URLs; adds GET `/authenticate` to exchange the query token for a 60-minute session and redirect to `/dashboard`; adds GET `/dashboard` to authenticate the stored session JWT and return `You're logged in.` for status 200; starts the server; sends POST `/login` with an email; receives the magic link; and clicks it to finish login or signup. The account fields, verification, workspace creation, initial project choice, and credential-arrival transitions before these documented actions remain unavailable.
candidate_path_gap.impact: Neither the complete account step nor a universal implementation path can be counted without inventing hidden states and choosing among peer architecture and tenant models.

branches.path: Select React, Next.js, or Vanilla JS and follow the relevant prebuilt login/signup component guide using the environment public_token, enabled Frontend SDKs, and an authorized domain.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Uses a prebuilt UI and frontend session hooks instead of backend routes and secret-key authentication.

branches.path: Select a supported web or mobile framework and build the UI using the headless frontend SDK route.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds developer-owned UI state and changes SDK, configuration, and testing steps.

branches.path: Select Node, Python, Go, Ruby, or Java and implement server-side login, callback, and protected-session handling with project_id and secret.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Uses server credentials and backend routes; exact installation and code differ by language.

branches.path: Follow the organization/member authentication route rather than Consumer Authentication.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes core entities, API surface, login discovery behavior, and terminal data.

friction_gates.description: The developer must select implementation type, framework, and Consumer versus Multi-tenant Authentication before Stytch exposes a concrete guide.
A Stytch account is required before API keys or public tokens are available.
The client-rendered dashboard hides the public account, workspace, and initial-project transitions.
Each selected guide requires environment-specific credentials and project configuration before code execution.

time_to_first_success.value: under 10 minutes for the guided authentication quickstart

sources.title: Quickstart
sections_used: Select the type of authentication for your project

sources.title: Stytch - User infrastructure + passwordless authentication
evidence_supported: Absence of public signup and project-convergence states

sources.title: Via backend SDK or API
sections_used: Install the Node.js SDK and configure API keys
Set up your app and login routes
Add a route to handle Stytch's redirect callback

sources.title: Authentication and authorization
evidence_supported: Vendor claim that the first auth flow can work in under 10 minutes

uncertainties.question: What exact identity methods, fields, verification, legal acceptance, workspace creation, project initialization, and post-submit states currently lead from Stytch signup to project credentials?
uncertainties.impact: The mandatory account and project portion cannot be reconstructed or counted.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The API Keys page says to sign up and the dashboard response is a client-rendered application shell without public signup states.

uncertainties.question: Which implementation type, framework, and authentication project type should define Stytch's universal first mile?
uncertainties.impact: Credentials, configuration, code, user interaction, and terminal state differ materially.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official Quickstart explicitly asks the developer to make all three choices and does not rank one combination as universal.

excluded_after_success: No complete end-to-end success is claimed while the account and initial project transition remains hidden.

Production-domain authorization, Live credentials, additional authentication methods, MFA, SSO, SCIM, RBAC, fraud controls, production session policy, and deployment occur after the first working authentication flow.
